data.title: Upbound outlines 2026 EPS of $4-$4.35 while Acima targets GMV flat to low single-digit growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Upbound Group (UPBD) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “Our first quarter represented a solid start to 2026 for Upbound. We executed well in a difficult operating environment, delivered results in line with our financial targets, generated robust cash flow and deleveraged our balance sheet” (CEO & Director Fahmi Karam).

  • “With our Brigit acquisition last year, we have 3 complementary brands that deliver a wide range of financial solutions to a similar and sizable target consumer base” and management is focused on “using data, advanced analytics and AI to improve personalization, strengthen underwriting and enhance operating efficiency across our organization” (CEO & Director Karam).

  • “We’ve continued investing in key senior leadership roles and talent and we’re thrilled to welcome our new Chief Technology Officer, Balaji Kumar” (CEO & Director Karam).

  • “Lease charge-offs were approximately 8.8% in the first quarter, representing a meaningful improvement… including a 130 basis point improvement compared to the fourth quarter,” while “GMV finished the quarter below our expectations” (CEO & Director Karam).

  • “Revenue was $1.2 billion, up 3.7% year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA increased nearly 8% to $136 million and non-GAAP diluted EPS was $1.08” (CEO & Director Karam).

  • “Quarter end liquidity was approximately $465 million… Net debt was approximately $1.4 billion and leverage was 2.6x trailing 12-month adjusted EBITDA” (Executive VP & CFO Hal Khouri).

Outlook

  • “We believe that we are well positioned to achieve the target ranges we shared for 2026 revenue, adjusted EBITDA and non-GAAP diluted EPS on our previous earnings call… consolidated revenue of approximately $4.7 billion to $4.95 billion, adjusted EBITDA of $500 million to $535 million and non-GAAP diluted earnings per share of $4 to $4.35” (CFO Khouri).

  • “We also expect free cash flow of approximately $200 million in 2026,” and the guidance “is inclusive in an estimated 2026 payment outflow of approximately $70 million in non-ordinary course legal and regulatory settlements and assumes relatively flat CapEx spend” (CFO Khouri).

  • Acima outlook was revised: “We expect 2026 GMV and revenue to be flat to up to low single digits year-over-year,” with losses “stabilizing in the low 9% area,” and “Acima adjusted EBITDA margin to finish the year up slightly relative to 2025” (CFO Khouri).

  • Q2 guidance: “consolidated revenue of $1.1 billion to $1.2 billion, adjusted EBITDA of $120 million to $130 million and non-GAAP diluted earnings per share of $1 to $1.10” (CFO Khouri).

Financial results

  • Segment detail included Brigit: “Revenue was $68 million,” “monthly ARPU… increased nearly 12% year-over-year to $14.41,” “Paying users were approximately 1.6 million,” and “adjusted EBITDA… approximately $22.9 million” (CFO Khouri).

  • Acima: “First quarter revenue was $649 million,” “GMV was approximately $427 million,” “Acima lease charge-offs were approximately 8.8%,” and “Adjusted EBITDA for Acima was $89 million… adjusted EBITDA margin was 13.7%” (CFO Khouri).

  • Rent-A-Center: “First quarter revenue was $482 million,” “same-store sales increasing approximately 40 basis points,” “lease charge-offs were approximately 4.7%,” and “Adjusted EBITDA for Rent-A-Center was $67 million” (CFO Khouri).

Q&A

  • Kyle Joseph, Stephens: asked about tax refunds and gas prices’ quarter cadence; CEO Karam said “fuel prices… puts a lot of pressure on their discretionary spending” and “tax season… came in about 10% on average a little higher,” while “people… didn’t exercise the payout options as much,” and noted Brigit Q1 seasonality: “we take a light on the marketing spend” (CEO Karam).

  • Kyle Joseph, Stephens: asked timing of Acima underwriting changes; CEO Karam said “we really started tightening… in the second quarter of last year” and added “by the second half of this year, we should return to growth at GMV” (CEO Karam).

  • Robert Griffin, Raymond James: pressed on what drove Acima GMV decline and whether anything else was “bleeding off”; CEO Karam said “the majority of what we saw… is around the underwriting tightness” and cited jewelry tightening: “that was down probably low to mid-teens” (CEO Karam).

  • Vincent Caintic, BTIG: asked about leadership hiring and Brigit turnover/earnouts; CEO Karam said founders “are going to transition into more of an advisory and consulting role in 2026” and framed it as “a natural evolution,” while CFO Khouri added the company is adding expertise “particularly in the areas of digital technology… AI, underwriting” (CEO Karam; CFO Khouri).

  • Anthony Chukumba, Loop Capital: asked about the large e-commerce furniture retailer agreement; CEO Karam said “The revised agreement enhances our integration and provides Acima exclusive rights as a checkout option” and later clarified exclusivity is “just on having the checkout button” (CEO Karam).

  • John Hecht, Jefferies: asked on deleveraging pace; CFO Khouri said “we’re contemplating paying that off and then aggressively paying down the debt,” but “there’s no real clock on that” (CFO Khouri).

  • Bradley Thomas, KeyBanc: asked about underwriting buffer and Brigit sensitivity; CEO Karam said “we remain highly disciplined… conservative,” and on Brigit: “people are feeling that pressure and need that extra cash and that extra liquidity” (CEO Karam).

  • William Reuter, BofA: asked how quickly fuel spikes hit behavior; CEO Karam said it was “noticeable” in “lower payouts” but “wasn’t an immediate shock” (CEO Karam).

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on GMV drivers, underwriting conservatism, product rollout timing, and legal accruals, including “how much of the GMV decline is from the tightening actions versus anything else” (Robert Griffin, Raymond James).

  • Management tone was slightly positive and disciplined, repeatedly emphasizing conservatism and playbooks, including “we remain highly disciplined… conservative in our posture” (CEO Karam).

  • Versus last quarter, management maintained similar confidence on 2026 consolidated targets, but used more revision language for Acima, stating “we revised our outlook” for flatter GMV growth (CFO Khouri).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • 2026 consolidated targets were reiterated in both calls: “consolidated revenue of approximately $4.7 billion to $4.95 billion… non-GAAP diluted earnings per share of $4 to $4.35” (CFO Khouri, Q1 2026; CFO Khouri, Q4 2025).

  • Acima guidance language shifted from growth to flatter: Q4 call said “2026 GMV and revenue to increase mid-single digits,” while Q1 call said “flat to up to low single digits year-over-year” (CFO Khouri, Q4 2025; CFO Khouri, Q1 2026).

  • Leadership build-out progressed: Q4 call highlighted new CFO and Chief Growth Officer, while Q1 call added “our new Chief Technology Officer, Balaji Kumar” (CEO Karam, Q4 2025; CEO Karam, Q1 2026).

Risks and concerns

  • Management cited consumer pressure: “elevated costs in essential categories such as groceries, rent, utilities and energy… weighs on discretionary spending” (CEO Karam).

  • Acima volume risk was linked to credit posture and macro: “Tightening underwriting, coupled with macro headwinds… pressured our GMV” (CEO Karam).

  • Legal and regulatory cash outflows remained in guidance: “estimated 2026 payment outflow of approximately $70 million in non-ordinary course legal and regulatory settlements” (CFO Khouri).

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as “in line with our financial targets” while highlighting improved lease charge-offs at Acima alongside lower-than-expected GMV, and reiterated full-year 2026 consolidated targets (revenue $4.7 billion to $4.95 billion, adjusted EBITDA $500 million to $535 million, non-GAAP EPS $4 to $4.35, free cash flow about $200 million). Executives emphasized a conservative underwriting posture, accelerated digital/AI execution, continued deleveraging (2.6x leverage at quarter-end), and near-term catalysts including the broader Brigit line of credit rollout later in the year, an expanded e-commerce checkout partnership expected to drive GMV in the second half, and the Amazon pickup/returns rollout across more than 1,700 Rent-A-Center stores starting in June.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: Roblox Q1 revenue, guidance falls short while bookings beat estimates
Granite projects $5.2B-$5.4B 2026 revenue while adding Kenny Seng Construction at ~$150M annually
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Granite Construction Incorporated (GVA) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We recently announced the acquisition of Kenny Seng Construction.” (President, CEO & Director Kyle Larkin) “We expect Kenny Seng Construction to add approximately $150 million in revenue annually with an accretive adjusted EBITDA margin in the high teens.”
  • “We ended the quarter with CAP of $7.2 billion, a $200 million increase from the fourth quarter.” (President, CEO & Director Larkin) “CAP increased despite a reduction of approximately $300 million related to the cancellation of a public sector highway project in California, where expanded scope exceeded available funding.”
  • “I believe that our federal business is positioned to generate more than 15% of our Construction segment revenue as we continue to grow this part of our business.” (President, CEO & Director Larkin) “Second, we are seeing growing opportunities in mission-critical data center projects.”
  • “Revenue increased 30% to $912 million.” (Executive VP & CFO Staci Woolsey) “Adjusted net income increased by $12 million to end at $12 million and adjusted EBITDA increased by $30 million to arrive at $58 million.”

Outlook

  • “We are increasing our revenue guidance to a range of $5.2 billion to $5.4 billion from a range of $4.9 billion to $5.1 billion.” (Executive VP & CFO Woolsey) “This increase reflects an additional $200 million of revenue from our new tactical infrastructure contract and $100 million in revenue from Kenny Seng Construction.”
  • “We are decreasing our SG&A as a percent of revenue guidance to a range of 8.25% to 8.75%, down from a range of 8.5% to 9%, inclusive of approximately $48 million in stock-based compensation expense.” (Executive VP & CFO Woolsey)
  • “We are also increasing our adjusted EBITDA margin guidance to a range of 12.25% to 13.25%, up from 12% to 13%.” (Executive VP & CFO Woolsey) “Finally, our CapEx guidance of $140 million to $160 million and our estimated adjusted effective tax rate in the mid-20s remain unchanged.”

Financial Results

  • “In the Construction segment, revenue increased $151 million or 25% year-over-year to $766 million.” (Executive VP & CFO Woolsey) “Of the growth in the quarter, $43 million came from the acquired businesses and the remaining $108 million was organic.”
  • “Materials segment revenue increased $61 million year-over-year to $146 million, with gross profit up $9 million to end at $8 million.” (Executive VP & CFO Woolsey) “Cash gross profit increased $15 million year-over-year to $26 million or 18% of revenue.”
  • “We used $31 million in operating cash in the quarter compared to an inflow of $4 million in the prior year.” (Executive VP & CFO Woolsey) “Our operating cash flow expectation of approximately 10% of revenue for the year remains unchanged.”
  • “In the first quarter, we completed privately negotiated transactions to settle $100 million principal amount of our convertible bonds that were scheduled to mature in 2028, leaving $274 million outstanding.” (Executive VP & CFO Woolsey) “Following the quarter, we utilized our revolving credit facility to fund the purchase of Kenny Seng Construction, and we now have $1.4 billion of debt outstanding and $415 million available under our revolving credit facility.”

Q&A

  • Steven Ramsey, Thompson Research Group, LLC: “Can you talk about the growth story that you can bring to KSC on a revenue or margin basis…?” President Larkin: “I would say there's really 3 things… One is we can support their scale… their materials business is an opportunity for us to also scale and grow… [and] share each other's client base.”
  • Steven Ramsey, Thompson Research Group, LLC: “The SG&A leverage, is there any way to break that out…?” Executive VP Woolsey: “With our SG&A change in guidance… it's being driven a lot by the revenue increase… about $200 million coming from the tactical infrastructure job and $100 million… from Kenny Seng Construction.”
  • Michael Dudas, Vertical Research Partners, LLC: asked about federal mix and differences. President Larkin: “We started off with revenues in that space of less than 5%… grown it up to around 10%… and now… right around that 15%.”
  • Kevin Gainey, Thompson, Davis & Company, Inc.: asked about the California cancellation and CAP durability. President Larkin: “That project in California is very unique… the scope exceeded the available funding… It is unusual. I think the project will come back I'm not sure in what form and what size.”
  • Adam Bubes, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc.: asked about risk controls on tactical infrastructure projects. President Larkin: “the risk… in 3 categories… schedule… remoteness… [and] the subcontractors and suppliers… we're being very selective about the partners that we have on those projects.”
  • Adam Bubes, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc.: asked about energy-cost sensitivity. President Larkin: “It's hard to provide a number… But what I can tell you is we've done a really nice job… And if anything, it's more positive than negative.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive, emphasizing execution and integration while probing cancellations, federal mix durability, project risk controls, and energy exposure, including “what are the chances that job comes back?” (Kevin Gainey, Thompson, Davis & Company, Inc.).
  • Management: positive and confident, pairing guidance actions with risk framing, including “We raised our 2026 guidance this quarter.” (President, CEO & Director Larkin) and “we do not presently expect that the current increases in oil prices will have a significant impact to our annual outlook.” (President, CEO & Director Larkin).
  • Versus last quarter: management language shifted from setting 2026 targets to reporting an early-year raise, while analysts rotated from IIJA timing and federal pipeline timing to tactical-infrastructure execution risk, California cancellation details, and integration of a new acquisition.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language moved from initiating 2026 ranges in Q4 to raising 2026 guidance in Q1, with CFO Woolsey stating the new revenue range and a higher adjusted EBITDA margin range.
  • Strategy emphasis broadened from Q4’s discussion of best value procurement and long-cycle portfolio quality to Q1’s added focus on tactical infrastructure wins, a new acquisition in Utah, and scaling federal contribution.
  • Analyst focus moved from IIJA renewal cadence and border pipeline timing in Q4 to Q1 diligence on tactical project risk parameters, the California CAP cancellation, and quantifying SG&A leverage from incremental revenue.

Risks and Concerns

  • “CAP increased despite… the cancellation of a public sector highway project in California, where expanded scope exceeded available funding.” (President, CEO & Director Larkin) “While cancellation of a project in CAP can occur… it is very rare in our experience.”
  • “Oil prices increased due to the conflict in Iran.” (President, CEO & Director Larkin) “We regularly work to mitigate exposure… [including] fixed forward contracts… financial hedges and… energy surcharges.”
  • “the risk… in 3 categories… schedule… remoteness… [and] the subcontractors and suppliers.” (President, CEO & Director Larkin) “we're being very selective about the partners that we have on those projects.”

Final Takeaway

Granite management highlighted a Q1 start that included a raised 2026 revenue range to $5.2 billion to $5.4 billion and higher adjusted EBITDA margin guidance of 12.25% to 13.25%, alongside a Utah acquisition expected to contribute about $150 million of annual revenue and “high teens” adjusted EBITDA margin. Management also underscored record CAP of $7.2 billion despite a rare California project cancellation tied to funding constraints, while describing continued growth ambitions in federal work, rail, and mission-critical data centers and outlining mitigation tools for energy volatility and tactical infrastructure execution risks.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Exchange Bank reports Q1 results
data.description:
• Exchange Bank press release (EXSR): For the quarter ending March 31, 2026, the Bank had net income after taxes of $7.51 million compared with net income of $5.61 million in 2025.
  • The Bank’s net interest income, which is the result of the Bank’s gross interest income net of interest expense, increased from $20.91 million during the first quarter of 2025, to $24.43 million for the first quarter of 2026, an increase of 16.84%. A component of this related to an increase in interest income year over year by $2.25 million primarily due to an increase in interest and fees on loans of $2.44 million offset by a decrease in interest from investment securities of $0.19 million. Total interest expense has decreased by $1.27 million. In the first quarter of 2026, total funding costs are made up of interest paid to depositors of $8.04 million and $0.40 million paid on borrowings.

    Non-interest income for the quarter ended March 31, 2026 was $6.33 million, consistent with the first quarter of 2025.

  • Non-interest expense remained relatively constant, increasing by 4.27% from the first quarter of 2025 to $20.63 million for the quarter ended March 31, 2026 compared to $19.78 million in the first quarter of 2025.

data.title: Option Care Health forecasts 2026 revenue of $5.675B-$5.775B while maintaining EBITDA of $480M-$505M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Option Care Health (OPCH) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "The first quarter reflected mixed performance for our business. Adjusted EBITDA and adjusted EPS performance were aligned with our expectations, but our revenue growth of 1% did not meet our expectations." (CEO, President & Director John Rademacher)
  • "Across our autoimmune and Chronic Inflammatory Portfolio, which we refer to as [ CID ]. We saw a greater reset than anticipated in patient census." (CEO Rademacher)
  • "We are revising our full year revenue guidance as the industry dynamics are more impactful than anticipated." (CEO Rademacher)
  • "Our first quarter revenue was $1.4 billion, up slightly over 1% compared to last year." (Executive VP & CFO Meenal Sethna)
  • "During the quarter, we also expanded our revolving credit facility to enhance financial flexibility from $400 million to $850 million." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)

Outlook

  • "We are adjusting our full year net revenue guidance to a range of $5.675 billion to $5.775 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "We are maintaining our full year EBITDA and adjusted EPS ranges with our February guidance with projected EBITDA of $480 million to $505 million and adjusted EPS range of $1.82 to $1.92." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "We are adjusting our operating cash flow target to at least $320 million, which incorporates the lower revenue and cash-based EBITDA reduction." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "We expect second quarter sequential revenue growth in the mid-single digits with EBITDA sequential growth in the high single digits." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted EBITDA of $105 million was down 6% over prior year, but in line with our expectations." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "Adjusted EPS of $0.40 was flat with prior year with an uplift of $0.02 from the year-over-year benefit of share repurchases." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "Operating cash flow for the quarter was a usage of $12 million, in line with our seasonal expectations." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "With clarity of those CID portfolio resets, we now estimate an approximately $55 million gross profit headwind for the year." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)

Q&A

  • "Can you help me to understand the increase in the headwind versus the initial on the gross profit side?" Lisa Gill, JPMorgan Chase & Co: Research Division; "this $55 million now represents... the change in the patient census itself... but then also therapy mix." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "I'm looking at teens sequential growth in 3Q and 4Q and how we accelerate from... 2Q." Pito Chickering, Deutsche Bank AG: Research Division; "we have a normal seasonal pattern... we tend to see sequential growth starting from the first quarter... up to the fourth quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "Was it just competitors were processing these folks a little bit more quickly than you guys and you were losing a little bit of share?" David MacDonald, Truist Securities, Inc.: Research Division; "we expect that we lost some of the patients to some of the competitors through that process." (CEO Rademacher)
  • "It seems like this is a market share loss situation... how can you impart confidence... to have visibility into guidance for the year?" Brian Tanquilut, Jefferies LLC: Research Division; "we reaffirm, yes, we lost those patients to other service providers." (CEO Rademacher)
  • "Are we still talking about Stelara... Or are we talking about... ENTYVIO...?" Joanna Gajuk, BofA Securities: Research Division; "it's primarily around the shift of the Stelara patient census." (CEO Rademacher)
  • "Would you still expect now that there's any risk transitioning into 2027...?" Michelle Foley, Morgan Stanley: Research Division; "no, we don't expect additional headwind in 2026 nor any carryover in 2027." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "Will that kind of just be on the back burner... as you focus on getting back to that stronger revenue growth?" James Kurek, UBS Investment Bank: Research Division; "the short answer is no... We're absolutely still committed to M&A activity." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)

Sentiment Analysis

  • "It seems like this is a market share loss situation" and "how can you impart confidence" reflected a slightly negative, pressing analyst tone centered on visibility, competitiveness, and the durability of guidance. (Brian Tanquilut, Jefferies LLC: Research Division)
  • "I am not satisfied with our revenue growth momentum" paired with "we remain confident in the strength of our platform" reflected a neutral-to-slightly defensive management tone, while repeatedly emphasizing clarity after the Q1 reset and sequential rebuilding. (CEO Rademacher)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s "we're excited to share updates" and "we are reaffirming the guidance" tone, the current call shifted toward explaining a "greater reset than anticipated" and a revised revenue outlook. (CEO Rademacher)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • "We are adjusting our full year net revenue guidance to a range of $5.675 billion to $5.775 billion" followed Q4 guidance of "full year 2026 revenue of $5.8 billion to $6 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "We are maintaining our full year EBITDA and adjusted EPS ranges" remained consistent with Q4 guidance of "adjusted EBITDA of $480 million to $505 million" and "adjusted diluted EPS of $1.82 to $1.92." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "We had previously estimated... $25 million to $35 million" and "we now estimate an approximately $55 million gross profit headwind" contrasted with Q4 language that the Stelara impact would "pattern out fairly evenly throughout the year" at the lower range. (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)

Risks and Concerns

  • "We saw a significantly higher volume of patients that had insurance plan, benefit design or formulary management changes, doubling the number of patients requiring benefit reverification and reauthorization versus last year." (CEO Rademacher)
  • "Across our rare and orphan program portfolio, we were also notified of launch delays or slower ramp for a few of our rare and orphan programs due to regulatory or commercial launch readiness." (CEO Rademacher)
  • "We expect that we lost some of the patients to some of the competitors through that process" and "there were some denials" described both competitive and payer-driven friction affecting retention and mix. (CEO Rademacher)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a "mixed performance" quarter where revenue growth "did not meet our expectations," driven by a sharper-than-modeled CID reset tied primarily to "the shift of the Stelara patient census," payer-driven reverification friction, and an unfavorable mix. The company lowered full-year revenue guidance to $5.675 billion-$5.775 billion and raised the expected CID-related gross profit headwind to about $55 million, while keeping EBITDA and adjusted EPS ranges unchanged and describing cost actions, commercial reinvestment, and sequential rebuilding as the path forward.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Quanta projects 2026 revenue of $34.7B-$35.2B while raising adjusted EPS view to $13.55-$14.25
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Quanta Services (PWR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Quanta is off to a strong start of the year with our first quarter results reflecting robust double-digit growth in revenues, adjusted EBITDA and adjusted earnings per share, along with record backlog.” (President, CEO & Director Earl Austin)
  • “What ran through everything we presented in our Investor Day was one word, "certainty", execution certainty, labor certainty, supply chain certainty, schedule certainty.” (President, CEO & Director Austin)
  • “On the fourth quarter call, we announced an investment of $500 million to $700 million over the next several years in our power transformer manufacturing facilities and vertical supply chain strategy, which will double our power transformer manufacturing capacity.” (President, CEO & Director Austin)
  • “This morning, we reported first quarter results with revenues of $7.9 billion, net income attributable to common stock of $221 million or $1.45 per diluted share, adjusted diluted earnings per share of $2.68 and adjusted EBITDA of $686 million.” (Chief Financial Officer Jayshree Desai)
  • “Based on the continued momentum evidenced by our record $48.5 billion of backlog, the strong performance during the quarter and improved visibility into the remainder of the year, we are raising our full year financial expectations.” (Chief Financial Officer Desai)

Outlook

  • “We now expect revenues to range between $34.7 billion to $35.2 billion, adjusted EBITDA to range between $3.49 billion to $3.65 billion, and adjusted EPS to range between $13.55 and $14.25.” (Chief Financial Officer Desai)
  • “Our guidance is prudent. It has always been prudent.” (President, CEO & Director Austin)
  • “Any acquisitions we do on a go-forward basis be additive to the guidance that we give… we’re not contemplating any of that in what you see today.” (President, CEO & Director Austin)

Financial Results

  • “This morning, we reported first quarter results with revenues of $7.9 billion… net income attributable to common stock of $221 million… adjusted diluted earnings per share of $2.68 and adjusted EBITDA of $686 million.” (Chief Financial Officer Desai)
  • “We gave a good range when we guided in the fourth quarter about our free cash flow… we didn’t feel the need… to change that with the $500 million range that we provided you guys in the fourth quarter.” (Chief Financial Officer Desai)
  • “Do I feel like we have greater confidence about being at the higher end of that cash flow range? Yes, I do.” (Chief Financial Officer Desai)

Q&A

  • Nicholas Amicucci, Evercore ISI: Asked if Underground & Infrastructure margin improvement was “a pull forward.” President, CEO & Director Austin: “I do think it continues to get higher and we have the ability to operate in double digits.”
  • Andrew Kaplowitz, Citigroup: Asked if backlog gains were driven by large-load awards. President, CEO & Director Austin: “It was broad-based… I expect our backlog to continue to rise.”
  • Brian Russo, Jefferies: Asked about NiSource GenCo expansion and timing into backlog. President, CEO & Director Austin: “None of the… generation… that’s been announced is not in our backlog… you’ll start to see that come into backlog, probably the later half of the year and beyond.”
  • Ati Modak, Goldman Sachs: Asked whether Quanta will “lean into” gas generation. President, CEO & Director Austin: “It depends on the risk… we’re going to build them under contract structure that makes sense for us and the client and the ratepayers.”
  • Sangita Jain, KeyBanc: Asked why free cash flow guidance was unchanged. Chief Financial Officer Desai: “Really nothing to read into that… it’s just early.”
  • Philip Shen, ROTH: Challenged Q1 strength vs full-year raise. Chief Financial Officer Desai: “Most of that was EBITDA strength for the year as well as a little bit of a tax beat that we carry forward.”
  • Charles Albert Dillard, Bernstein: Asked what it would take to do “full turnkey data center builds at scale.” President, CEO & Director Austin: “If our clients push us that way and asked us to do that, we can do it… if we do it at scale, we need to add.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts tone was slightly skeptical, pressing for durability and conversion, including questions on whether margin upside was “pull forward,” when announced generation spend will reach backlog, and why guidance increases were smaller than perceived Q1 outperformance.
  • Management tone was slightly positive and controlled, repeatedly emphasizing prudence and risk management, including “Our guidance is prudent” (President, CEO & Director Austin) and “we’re going to be prudent about how we take risk” on combined-cycle generation (President, CEO & Director Austin).
  • Versus Q4 2025, management messaging leaned more on Investor Day execution and raised full-year expectations, while Q&A stayed focused on backlog composition, gas generation risk, and scaling data center scope rather than acquisition integration.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q1 2026, the company shifted from setting a baseline 2026 outlook to explicitly “raising our full year financial expectations” on “improved visibility into the remainder of the year.” (Chief Financial Officer Desai)
  • Discussion of vertical integration broadened: Q1 2026 reiterated transformer investment and added that Quanta is “nearly doubling our off-site manufacturing, fabrication and logistics facilities… approximately 6.7 million square feet.” (President, CEO & Director Austin)
  • Analyst focus moved from margin steadiness, supply chain, and renewables noise (Q4 2025) toward backlog drivers, interconnection timelines, gas generation risk controls, and whether Quanta can expand into larger-scope data center delivery (Q1 2026).

Risks and Concerns

  • On combined-cycle gas plants, management stressed execution and contract risk: “We’ll be prudent about how we take risk on them.” (President, CEO & Director Austin)
  • On grid constraints and interconnection delays, management pointed to equipment lead times and permitting: “The transformer manufacturing investment matters a ton when you start talking about 36 months on transformers.” (President, CEO & Director Austin)
  • On geopolitics and commodity volatility, management said: “Our guidance contemplates anything like that… I’m not seeing anything that would impact our customer or ourselves at this point.” (President, CEO & Director Austin)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 2026 as an execution-led start to a multi-year plan centered on “certainty,” supported by record $48.5 billion backlog and a raised 2026 outlook for revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and adjusted EPS. In Q&A, management repeatedly emphasized risk-adjusted growth—particularly for combined-cycle generation—and positioned vertical supply chain and off-site manufacturing expansion as key enablers for faster, more reliable delivery across utility, generation, and large-load/data center demand.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Elon Musk testifies he "didn't read the fine print" in OpenAI case
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) and SpaceX (SPACE) CEO Elon Musk told a federal court on Thursday that he "didn't read the fine print" on a term sheet that had been forwarded to him by OpenAI (OPENAI) CEO Sam Altman in 2017 regarding converting the AI company from a non-profit to a for-profit entity overseen by a non-profit.

"My testimony is I didn't read the fine print, just the headline," Musk said, according to Reuters, during his third day of testimony for a lawsuit he filed against OpenAI.

Musk, an OpenAI co-founder, is suing for $150B, according to Reuters, claiming he was manipulated into investing $38M in the company when it was a non-profit, only to have management convert it into a for-profit entity for their own benefit.

Co-defendants in the suit include OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and President Greg Brockman, who also co-founded the company. Microsoft (MSFT), a major investor in OpenAI, is also party to the suit. 

In addition to monetary damages, Musk is also asking the court to remove Altman and Brockman from their corporate roles and unwind the company's conversion to a for-profit entity.

OpenAI (OPENAI) has countered that Musk was aware the company might be converted into a for-profit entity at some point and decided to walk away in 2018 after failing to gain control. Musk went on to found his own AI company, xAI (X.AI), in 2023, which is now part of Musk's SpaceX (SPACE).

OpenAI has also asserted that Musk filed the suit in part to slow down its progress, given Musk's interest in xAI. On Thursday, Musk disclosed that his company has used OpenAI models to train xAI models. 

OpenAI's attorneys on Thursday questioned Musk as to why he thought OpenAI would remain a non-profit after receiving emails from other co-founders discussing how they could profit from the technology.

"I was reassured by Sam Altman and others ​that OpenAI would continue as a nonprofit," Musk replied, according to Reuters.

Both OpenAI and SpaceX are reportedly preparing to launch massive IPOs later this year.
